data.title: Ashley Westwood set to leave Hong Kong job days after disappointing Singapore defeat
data.description:
Ashley Westwood is set to leave his role as head coach of the Hong Kong football team, a source has told the Post.
Englishman Westwood’s position came into question after Hong Kong slumped to a disappointing 2-1 Asian Cup qualifying defeat to Singapore on Tuesday.
Supporters have clamoured for Westwood’s dismissal, while he was also criticised by the city’s sports minister, Rosanna Law Shuk-pui.
The Football Association of Hong Kong, China has stayed tight-lipped since the loss at Kai Tak Stadium, which sent Singapore to the 2027 finals at the expense of Hong Kong.
It is expected they will announce Westwood’s departure this afternoon, although it is unclear whether the 49-year-old has resigned or been pushed out. Appointed in August last year, Westwood won 10 and lost six of his 20 matches in charge.
More to follow …

data.title: Marcos’ cousin faces plunder charges in Philippine corruption scandal
data.description:
Plunder, corruption and bribery charges were recommended against Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr’s cousin for his alleged involvement in a corruption scandal.
The Department of Public Works and Highways sought these charges against former House speaker Martin Romualdez during a filing at the Office of the Ombudsman on Friday.
The same charges were also recommended for resigned lawmaker Zaldy Co, who was formerly head of the House of Representatives’ appropriations committee. Romualdez and Co have previously denied the allegations.
Marcos first exposed the misuse of state funds in July and vowed to root out corruption that involved state money meant to mitigate flooding in one of the world’s disaster-prone nations. The corruption scandal has angered the public and soured investor sentiment, dragging the economy to its slowest growth in four years.


Meanwhile, the Philippines on Thursday auctioned off seven luxury vehicles linked to the corruption scandal, as the government pushes to recover stolen public funds.
Among the cars up for auction were a Bentley Bentayga, a Rolls-Royce Cullinan and a Mercedes-Benz G63 AMG, according to the Bureau of Customs. The agency earlier seized allegedly smuggled vehicles from a contractor being investigated for involvement in the flood-control corruption scheme.
The auction, among the first moves to recover misappropriated funds from the scandal, is expected to yield a minimum of 110 million pesos (US$1.86 million), which will be remitted to the National Treasury, according to the customs agency.
“I know that’s not a big amount, but it’s important that we take the money back,” customs chief Ariel Nepomuceno said in a live-streamed speech.

data.title: Singapore minister’s apology, China’s tourists cancel Japan trips: SCMP’s 7 highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from this week’s news across Hong Kong, mainland China, the wider Asia region and beyond that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. 491,000 Japan-bound air tickets cancelled as China warns people to avoid travel
Chinese airlines have recorded about 491,000 cancellations of tickets to Japan since Saturday – roughly 32 per cent of their total bookings to the typically popular destination – after Beijing advised citizens to avoid travelling there amid a diplomatic spat, according to a veteran aviation analyst.
2. Singapore minister sorry for derogatory remarks on Hong Kong football team

Singapore’s acting sports minister has conceded he “should have been more respectful” after calling Hong Kong football fans and players “idiots” in a locker room pep talk to his side, following the city state’s historic win on Tuesday night in Hong Kong.
3. Mathematician Qian Hong, son of top scientific clan, leaves US for China
A renowned mathematician who is also part of China’s Qian clan – a surname linked in the annals of Chinese scientific history to national pioneers in science and engineering – has become the latest US-based scientist to return to China.
4. Chinese history blamed this woman for 2,796 years. Science clears her name

A groundbreaking scientific study published in the journal Communications Earth & Environment might compel historians to rewrite one of ancient China’s most enduring myths.
5. Hong Kong lawyer who admitted to sex with girl, 12, says relationship was genuine
A Hong Kong lawyer who admitted having sex with a 12-year-old girl has maintained that they were in a genuinely loving relationship. Kelvin Luk Kin-ting’s legal counsel on Monday sought to convince the District Court that the defendant, 40, had not groomed his victim to satisfy his sexual desires or taken naked photos of her for financial benefit.
6. China’s ‘coal plastic’ breakthrough can greatly reduce its demand for oil: study
Chinese scientists have developed a catalyst that can turn biomass and coal-derived syngas directly into building blocks for plastic and synthetic rubber with higher efficiency and sustainability than existing methods.
7. ‘Excellent’: Singapore lauded for jailing man who grabbed Ariana Grande

Singapore’s jailing of an Australian man who rushed at singer-actress Ariana Grande at a film premiere has earned near-unanimous applause from locals, reflecting deep frustration with behaviour seen as attention-seeking and disruptive to the city state’s hard-won image for safety.

data.title: One of the fastest Michelin stars ever – Hong Kong-born chef’s unique restaurant Udtryk
data.description:
Hong Kong-born chef Edward Lee opened his restaurant Udtryk (pronounced “oot-trehk”) in Copenhagen, Denmark, on May 7. Just 41 days later, the restaurant was awarded a Michelin star – one of the quickest on record.
Lee says winning the star was a huge surprise.
“It was an absolute shock to the system on the night,” says the 38-year-old chef. “It’s absolutely wonderful. To go from zero to one star so quickly without a big-name chef or anything, that’s something to be proud of.”
Lee may not yet be a big-name chef, but Udtryk has certainly got the culinary world talking.
The restaurant melds Chinese with Nordic, French and Japanese approaches. Named after the Danish word for “expression”, it is the articulation of Lee’s gastronomic personality developed over the past two decades on opposite sides of the world.

At Udtryk, he draws heavily on his Hong Kong heritage. He only lived in the city until the age of five, but he often returns to visit family. He grew up in Sydney but kept connected to Hong Kong’s food culture through Sydney’s substantial Chinatown and Chinese neighbourhoods. The move to Australia instilled in him a lifelong fascination for different cultures and cuisines.
It was in Sydney that he got his first job in a professional kitchen – as a dishwasher. The energy and camaraderie of the professional kitchen gave him a profound sense of belonging. He found joy working in an environment that made others feel welcome.
He enrolled in formal culinary education, training in classical French, and took multiple apprenticeships before joining the team at Claude’s, a celebrated French restaurant in Sydney.
Singapore-born chef Chui-lee Luk was introducing Asian flavours to the dishes at Claude’s at the time – something unusual two decades ago when modern European dishes from chefs such as Marco Pierre White and Gordon Ramsay were dominating the Sydney food scene, says Lee.
“My time at Claude’s showed me that you can cook Asian food in a refined environment in a very refined way. It was exceptionally inspiring,” he says.
After 37 years of service, Claude’s closed in 2013. Lee decided to expand his gastronomic horizons and left for Europe in 2014. He worked in top kitchens in the UK and Denmark, finessing his talent for modern European cuisine, becoming skilled in Nordic with its focus on local sourcing of ingredients and sustainability, and exploring the Japanese culinary lexicon loved by fine dining chefs in the West.

Udtryk is where he brings it all together.
“The key is in the name – Udtryk,” says Lee. “Some chefs, perhaps because of investors, are forced to cook in a certain way, or to follow a particular fad. But to me, it’s about learning from the masters, bottling it all up, and then expressing it in your own way.”
Throughout Udtryk’s 10-course seasonal tasting menu, Lee so seamlessly blends East and West that it is almost impossible to detect where one ends and the next begins.
A trio of small bites that begin the meal include a tiny tartlet of whipped, home-made tofu with white asparagus, cucumber, and the first of the season’s Danish peas. Then there is a croustade (pastry shell) with lobster claw marinated with Buddha’s hand (Asian fingered citron), paired with pickled magnolia and a jelly made from the lobster shells.

The beautifully refreshing dish of Norwegian trap-caught shrimp with new Danish green asparagus, nashi pears and yuzu juice comes before the impressive sustainably caught North Sea turbot, which is dry aged in-house for seven days. The fish is topped with a farce (stuffing) made from the tail and skirt of the turbot and 10-year-aged tangerine peel from Xinhui in southern China. The dish is served with a 10-year-old Shaoxing wine beurre blanc (butter sauce) on Royal Copenhagen plates.
Lee called in a favour from family in Hong Kong to get his hands on the prized tangerine peel.
“We’re the only ones who have this in Denmark,” he says. “I was inspired by the Hong Kong technique of using tangerine peel to minimise fishiness, and replaced the classic French combination of vin jaune with turbot with the Shaoxing wine as there were similar notes, although the Chinese wine has deeper, more sherry notes.”
Lee often looks for inspiration from Chinese food for more robust flavours, although not going as far as the very bold style of the Sichuan kitchen.
“I think the Cantonese style is very relevant to the Nordic and Japanese styles,” he says. “The ingredients play a big factor – we try to bring out the best in each ingredient.”
In the show-stopper final savoury dish, Lee ramps up the umami: koshihikari rice from Niigata, Japan, is cooked in an earthenware pot Lee imported from China. The rice is seasoned with an emulsion of lobster coral (the red cooked roe) and brain, and topped with sliced short rib from Denmark’s only Wagyu producer, Norlin, grilled maitake mushrooms and caviar.

On the side is a dumpling stuffed with chopped langoustine tail, duck liver and, supporting his low-waste ethos, minced meat from the legs of the quail served in the course before. The dumpling rests in a broth of Chinese double-boiled superior chicken and is served in a classic blue-and-white porcelain dish.
Dessert continues the multicultural kaleidoscope, with a palate-cleansing granita of milky oolong custard with melon, rose lychee and Amalfi lemon; a rhubarb, rose and ginger soufflé; and petit fours that mix white chocolate with yuzu, black garlic and licorice.
The culinary background of a dish or cooking technique – whether it is from the Chinese, Nordic, French or Japanese repertoire – is just one aspect Lee considers when planning his menu.
“The background is important for what you’re trying to convey in terms of a culinary message, but it also has to fit in within the menu flow. If I put a big typhoon-style fried chicken dish at the start, your palate is covered in salt and spice,” he says.
“We take it from a more classical perspective. I was always taught to start off lighter, more delicate, more mineral and then build it up from there. With too many explosive flavours, when you get to the eighth or ninth serving, your palate is over-dominated. We always position ourselves as guests to see how the journey evolves, from the palate to the storytelling to the culinary background of each dish.”

Lee has learned from a star-studded cast over the years. Most recently, he was assistant chef for Eric Vildgaard, chef-owner of Copenhagen’s Restaurant Jordnaer, known for its exquisite tasting menus of elegantly bold, internationally inflected Nordic dishes. During Lee’s tenure from 2018 to 2022, Jordnaer was awarded two Michelin stars; the restaurant has gone on to earn a third.
Lee also worked at the now-closed modern Nordic restaurant Studio, which was opened by Vildgaard’s brother, chef Torsten Vildgaard, who was previously head of research and development at Noma, and now helms FZN Dubai, which went straight to three Michelin stars in May.
Before settling in Denmark, Lee worked under Mikael Jonsson at the renowned modern European restaurant Hedone in London, now closed.
Udtryk is not Lee’s first restaurant. In 2022, he opened Gaijin, an affordable, ingredient-driven izakaya-style restaurant in Copenhagen, where, he says, there were limited options for Japanese food at the time. Gaijin closed in 2024; Lee cites issues with the investors.
“It wasn’t that we fell out of love with it or we didn’t want to do it any more. Things behind the scenes didn’t go according to plan,” he says.
Now he has a new investor, Bjarke Just Nielsen of the Norrlyst Group, which runs 14 other restaurants across Copenhagen. His romantic partner, Regitze Jensen, helps him run the restaurant as manager. Lee is thankful for the support: with the acquisition of the Michelin star, the restaurant is consistently packed.
“We’re running a full house for the next few months, it’s pretty full-on. We’re capping a few of the covers – my team has been working five days a week from 10am till close for the past month. We’re looking for new staff so we can cut the hours down to make sure everyone can still perform at a high level consistently without feeling exhausted,” he says.
“The goal is to make it a sustainable workplace with the ethos of ‘why not?’ I don’t want the restraint of ‘we must cook Japanese’ or ‘we must cook this style’. There’s never been more choices to eat. It’s never been a better time to be a chef.”

data.title: Japan’s export of lethal weapons to the US ‘extremely dangerous’: Chinese analysts
data.description:
Japan’s export of lethal weapons sent an “extremely dangerous signal” indicating expanded military capabilities, Chinese observers and media said on Thursday as relations between the two countries continued to nosedive.
The assessment followed Japanese media reports on Wednesday that Japan had exported domestically produced Patriot surface-to-air missile interceptors to the United States.
The US would use the missiles to replenish its own stockpile as it provided military support to Ukraine in the war against Russia, Kyodo News reported.
Japan’s export of lethal weapons has been heavily constrained by its Three Principles on Transfer of Defence Equipment and Technology, guidelines for the country’s arms exports. In recent years, Tokyo has repeatedly sought to revise the principles.
The efforts included a 2023 amendment to the principles permitting the supply of Japanese-made Patriot missiles to the US.

For years, Beijing has accused Japan’s political right-wing of whitewashing the nation’s wartime history and trying to amend its pacifist constitution, alter its non-nuclear policy and expand its military capabilities.
Global Times, a state-owned tabloid newspaper affiliated with the Communist Party mouthpiece People’s Daily, quoted Chinese military expert Zhang Xuefeng on Thursday as saying Japan “has no intention of exercising self-restraint in arms exports, which is an extremely dangerous signal that will trigger a series of chain reactions”.
Zhang argued that “Japan will use the export of Patriot missiles as a template to export more lethal weapons abroad, which will inevitably pose a threat to regional security”.
The recent weapons shipment comes in the midst of a diplomatic row between Beijing and Tokyo over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on a Taiwan contingency.
Speaking to the Japanese parliament on November 7, Takaichi stated that the use of force against Taiwan could be deemed a “survival-threatening situation”, thereby enabling Tokyo to deploy its Self-Defence Forces.
The Japanese leader has refused to retract her comments, further infuriating Beijing.
Talks in Beijing on Tuesday involving senior diplomats failed to ease the tensions, with Beijing reportedly imposing a seafood import ban and suspending cultural exchange events as well as intergovernmental dialogue.
Takaichi’s comments suggesting Japan might intervene should Beijing use force against Taiwan signalled a shift from the long-standing policy of “strategic ambiguity” voiced by previous Japanese leaders.


Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including Japan and the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Beijing has vowed to respond with “resolute” countermeasures if Tokyo were to resort to force in the Taiwan Strait.
Song Zhongping, a Chinese military commentator and former People’s Liberation Army instructor, said Japan wanted to leverage arms production for the US to advance its domestic military industry.
“It can replicate or even improve certain American weapons systems by introducing and acquiring intellectual property,” Song added.
Song said Japan hoped the shipment would create a “breach” in its strict arms export restrictions.
In addition, Japan was seeking to “exert political influence” by exporting arms to countries such as the Philippines and Australia, according to Song.
Tokyo’s ultimate objective, he said, was “to amend the Peace Constitution and normalise the nation” – terminology tied to improving Japan’s national defences.
Last month, Japan’s new defence minister, Shinjiro Koizumi, announced he would continue to promote the country’s arms exports.
Also last month, Takaichi said she intended to increase Japan’s defence spending to 2 per cent of GDP by the end of March next year, fully two years ahead of the deadline set by previous governments of her ruling Liberal Democratic Party.

data.title: Hong Kong’s laws targeting false credentials must apply equally
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
The recent case of Li Fei, who used false credentials to obtain an entry permit under Hong Kong’s Top Talent Pass Scheme, raises serious concerns about the city’s equal application of the law.
Despite presenting counterfeit documents, including a fake overseas degree, to claim benefits intended for genuine talent, Li faced no criminal conviction or custodial sentence. Instead, after the Department of Justice decided to withdraw prosecution, the court imposed only a two-year bind-over order with a bond of HK$2,000 (US$256).
A bind-over order is not a criminal penalty; no conviction is recorded and no punishment is imposed. In response to media queries, the Department of Justice declined to comment on individual cases. It stated that its bind-over orders are compliant with the Prosecution Code and serve the public interest. However, such explanation offers little reassurance, given the wider implications for public trust in Hong Kong’s legal and immigration systems.
This outcome stands in sharp contrast to a similar case earlier this year, when another woman, convicted of forging a Columbia University degree and falsifying academic records to enter the University of Hong Kong, was sentenced to 240 days’ imprisonment. Such disparity inevitably raises questions about consistency, transparency and the principle of equality before the law.
Allowing deliberate deception in immigration matters to go unpunished trivialises the seriousness of such offences and risks eroding confidence in Hong Kong’s rule of law. I urge the Department of Justice to review its prosecution policies to ensure that deterrence, fairness and consistency remain the guiding principles in every case. Only by doing so can our city maintain public faith in the integrity and impartiality of its justice system.
Herbert Kwok, Sha Tin
What Thatcher could teach Takaichi on dealing with China
A self-proclaimed admirer of Margaret Thatcher, Japan’s newly inaugurated prime minister Sanae Takaichi has consciously channelled her role model by signalling her intent to govern with steely conviction and even adopting her role model’s signature look of pairing a pearl necklace with blue suits.
However, in just one month, Takaichi’s hardline rhetoric regarding Taiwan and Japan’s defence policy has swiftly ignited the fury of the Chinese government and people, resulting in an escalating diplomatic row.
The controversy centres on her November 7 statement in the Japanese Diet that an emergency in Taiwan involving force could constitute a situation that threatens Japan’s survival, thereby allowing Japan to exercise self-defence. Despite drawing fire with her remarks, Takaichi, often dubbed Japan’s “Iron Lady”, has refused to retract the remarks.
Her situation arguably mirrors the 1982 confrontation between Thatcher and Chinese leader Deng Xiaoping over Hong Kong. Fresh from the UK’s victory in the Falklands War, Thatcher initially insisted on retaining British administration post 1997 after acknowledging China’s sovereignty. However, Deng’s position was unwavering: sovereignty was non-negotiable and China would reclaim all of Hong Kong.
Ultimately, Thatcher pivoted from ideology to pragmatism, conceding administration and signing the Sino-British Joint Declaration in 1984, which paved the path for Hong Kong’s handover in 1997. Though a fierce opponent, she was a pragmatist who respected and eventually accepted the historical inevitability of Hong Kong’s return to China.
In contrast to her role model’s pragmatism, Takaichi’s recklessness has sparked domestic backlash. Former prime minister Yoshihiko Noda criticised her for her “rash” remarks, while former prime minister Yukio Hatoyama accused her of inciting a crisis that could justify military expansion. An editorial in Shakai Shimpo newspaper claimed it is Takaichi’s existence as prime minister itself that constitutes a survival crisis for Japan.
Takaichi may aspire to emulate Thatcher’s strength, but the diplomatic crisis she has created suggests she is failing to heed her hero’s most important lesson when dealing with China: sovereignty is the ultimate red line.
By challenging China’s core interest – Taiwan – Takaichi risks creating an unstable situation in the region, leading some critics to suggest she is closer to becoming the political equivalent of Britain’s other famous, but ultra-short-lived, prime minister, Liz Truss.
Tibby Chen, Beijing
No excuse for Trump’s insult of journalist
US President Donald Trump was questioned about the Epstein files by a reporter last week and he responded with “Quiet. Quiet, piggy”. This is wrong in so many ways.
It is OK to criticise what reporters write, not the reporters themselves. Without a free and reliable news media, a democracy won’t be able to operate safely.
Trump’s verbal assault on a person was also out of line. But who can forget his history of levelling insults at so many others, including lawyers and politicians?
Dennis Fitzgerald, Melbourne, Australia

data.title: China-made EV parts reportedly in Tesla’s crosshairs – but is a total ‘de-risk’ feasible?
data.description:
Tesla’s reported plan to stop using Chinese-made components in US-built cars aligns with a “significant strategy” in national industrial policy, with some American giants seeking to “completely de-risk” supply chains amid an increasingly tense geopolitical climate, an automotive executive said.
Curt Hopkins, CEO of automotive investment platform MCQ Markets in Miami, said Tesla appeared to be “developing a completely dual strategy, where in the US they’re going to de-China, diversify and make sure they continue to have access to government incentives”.
“And then in China, it [Tesla] is just fully localised, relying completely on Chinese battery and component system technology. They’re going to have two entirely different supply chain strategies for the cars.”
However, as electric vehicle (EV) technology in China rapidly advances and the US loses ground, he warned of a “big danger”: the possibility that the Chinese side could dominate the entire technology stack.
According to Tesla Mag, the EV giant plans to issue requirements forcing suppliers to exclude Chinese components from vehicles built in the US. A complete phase-out is anticipated within the next one or two years to mitigate the impact of tariffs and trade disputes.
Tesla’s relatively mature manufacturing capacity and “established strategy” could “probably create this divide”, Hopkins said, noting that it would have to reshore everything and scale up battery capacity.
“They are one of the most dynamic, fast-moving firms, especially when Elon Musk is incentivised to make the change, so he could force it through,” he added.
“Being 100 per cent free in two years is kind of big. If you are 80 per cent there in three years, it’s effectively the same thing.”


Hopkins expects Tesla to start winding down contracts with Chinese suppliers while pushing North American counterparts to cut dependence on Chinese components. Despite the challenges and risks facing the US multinational, he said the industry is closely watching whether Tesla’s strategy is successful, because “pleasing two masters” is difficult.
“If they can make that work, then it is a sign of what can be done,” he added.
“If you look at obviously what Apple’s doing, with their manufacturing and trying to reshore some of the capacity, it’d be very interesting to watch what they do and how they play that game and the dance there.”
Analysts said Tesla’s strategy has been in place for a while but has accelerated as supply chains enter the arena of geopolitical rivalry.
“Beyond tariffs, the new US foreign entity of concern rules, the willingness to denote companies like CATL as a ‘Chinese military company’, is reshaping supply chains with a quest for economic securitisation,” said Vivek Kelkar, an independent analyst based in Mumbai.
“Tesla will work with firms like Intel to bring critical production like chips – with ‘Tesla terafabs’, as Musk calls them – back to the US, but the critical mineral interdependence on China is harder to unshackle in the medium term.”
Kelkar said complete decoupling from Chinese tech, while a desirable end goal in the US-China rivalry, is “neither practically feasible nor truly cost-effective” given the centrality of the world’s second-largest economy in Asian manufacturing and Mexico’s role in Chinese strategy.
Yao Jin, an interim department chair in supply chain and operations management at Miami University, said costs would “definitely go up”, noting that it typically takes up to two years for a new supplier in the auto industry to pass all quality assessments.
“When Tesla tried to take a short cut years ago, they ended up having to remachine entire shipments of parts using expensive labour in California.”
Tesla could move a single tier of its supply chain out of China, with tier two and other operations still reliant on Chinese input components, Yao added.
Hopkins said costs would be “the biggest challenge”. Despite the show of “industrial strength” from the American side, he argued the automotive supply chain would not completely split and that the US has “a lot of work to do” to catch up with China’s edge in EV technology.

data.title: 5 of the best things to do in Hong Kong this weekend, November 21-23
data.description:
It is often said that Hong Kong does not have an autumn season. This year, October arrived with sweltering heat, and temperatures continued to linger above 20 degrees Celsius (68 degrees Fahrenheit) in early November. But with a cold front sweeping through the city this week, it finally feels like autumn is here.
As the weather cools, it could be the perfect time to visit an exhibition that allows some quiet introspection. Or maybe the cold makes you want to discover delicious, warming food items. If you simply want to see a new film, we have the perfect recommendation.
Here are five things to do in Hong Kong this weekend.
1. Yip Kai-chun x Peng Chau Cinema

For Hong Kong artist Yip Kai-chun, who has lived on Peng Chau island for more than eight years, the ferry is more than a vessel – taking it is a daily act of connection that bridges the island with the city.
“How to draw a line across the sea?” is a solo exhibition by Yip currently running at the Peng Chau Cinema until December 21.
In his exhibition, which is open for visitors from Thursdays to Sundays, Yip contemplates the boundaries between geography and memory through his collection of videos, photographs, sound and light installations, and found-object work.
The exhibition is one of several recent projects reviving the Peng Chau Cinema. Opened in the 1970s, the cinema was closed for more than 40 years until local groups began working to stage markets, screenings and exhibitions – now including Yip’s.
For visitors who want to book an after-hours appointment, registration details can be found here.
Peng Chau Cinema, 15 Wai Tsai Street, Peng Chau
2. SPCA Dog Meet & Greet in Wan Chai

As Hong Kong’s largest and longest-standing independent animal welfare charity, the SPCA is dedicated to promoting kindness and protecting animals. Right now, the SPCA has over 400 animals looking for their forever homes with Hong Kong families.
On Saturday from 10am to 12.30pm, 14 adoption-ready dogs, accompanied by dedicated SPCA staff and dog walkers, will participate in SPCA’s Dog Meet & Greet event in Wan Chai.
Siu Mai, Chui Chui and SpongeBob are just a few of the dogs waiting to show off their endearing personalities.
With free admission and no prior reservation needed, this open, stress-free event allows the dogs to interact playfully with the community. Pet lovers can go shower these rescue dogs with love, and they might just return home with a new furry friend.
SPCA Wan Chai Headquarters, 5 Wan Shing Street, Wan Chai
3. LMO Freshly Baked x Hansik Goo

LMO Freshly Baked at the Landmark is taking part in an exciting collaboration throughout November as part of its Tastemaker series.
It has partnered with one-Michelin-star Korean restaurant Hansik Goo, whose executive chef Park Seung-hun has curated a limited-time menu of four must-try dishes, bringing signature Korean flavours to the popular grab-and-go spot until November 30.
The menu includes spicy perilla chicken salad, rich seolleongtang beef bone soup, a unique galbi namul pie and a savoury gochujang chicken sandwich featuring perilla leaf pesto and mozzarella.
2/F, Landmark, 15 Queen’s Road Central, Central
4. Journey of Wellness at Central Market

This Saturday and Sunday, Central Market is hosting a series of wellness-related workshops in partnership with Hong Kong non-profit organisation The Elya Foundation.
On both days, from 10am to 6pm, Central Market’s Journey of Wellness will offer an array of therapeutic and restorative workshops. These will include sessions for Pilates, Zumba, gentle stretching and singing bowls – all for free.
Not only can city dwellers take part in low-stress fitness activities, but they can also listen to Hong Kong athletes and health professionals share their wellness stories.
Golfer Ginnie Ding and football coach Tim Bredbury will speak about their fitness journeys and how they balance life with sports, while traditional Chinese medicine professionals will share advice on managing mental health.
The essential oils workshop will teach participants about the oils’ herbal properties and holistic health benefits – those who take part will even get to go home with a little fragrant souvenir.
All classes are first-come, first-served. Registration details are available here.
1/F, Central Market, 93 Queen’s Road Central, Central
5. Wicked: For Good


The follow-up to Jon M. Chu’s Oscar-winning box office smash Wicked has just arrived at cinemas.
It is hard not to imagine Wicked: For Good doing as well as its prequel. It is a dazzling extravaganza anchored by two powerful performances from Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo.
While it does not quite have the novelty element of part one – or the emotional heft – Wicked: For Good is a fiercely inventive work that pays dutiful homage to its 1939 source, The Wizard of Oz.
Picking up where the last film left off, Elphaba (Erivo) is the banished, green-skinned Wicked Witch, her name smeared by the charlatan Wizard of Oz (Jeff Goldblum).
Meanwhile, the handsome Prince Fiyero (Jonathan Bailey) has been entrusted to capture Elphaba, even as his wedding to her friend Glinda the Good (Grande) approaches. Read our full review.
Wicked: For Good is in cinemas now.
Find this useful? Look out for our recommendations every Friday online.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs meets HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery
data.description:
Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs has met a delegation led by HSBC Group CEO Georges Elhedery, marking his second meeting with the bank’s leadership in eight months.
But the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office did not reveal what was discussed in the meeting between its director, Xia Baolong, and Elhedery in Beijing on Thursday.
Xu Qifang, the executive deputy director of the office and veteran HSBC banker Peter Wong Tung-shun had also attended the meeting.

During his meeting with group chairman Mark Tucker in April, Xia called on HSBC to act as an “important bridge” in Sino-British relations and promote both the country and the city to the international community.
Acknowledging the HSBC Group’s “long-term optimism over China and Hong Kong” and its active participation in the city’s economic and social development, he also called on the conglomerate at the time to play to its strengths and be more active in helping cement its role as an international financial centre.

data.title: Hong Kong plans demolition of 26 housing estates in 1985 – SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on November 21, 1985
By James Sun
Families to be evacuated
The Housing Authority is expected to announce today (November 21, 1985) an intensive redevelopment programme for 26 substandard estate blocks from which more than 17,000 families will be evacuated in the next four years.
The super programme, costing taxpayers up to $800 million will start next month if given the go-ahead by the Executive Council.
The Housing Authority is to meet this morning to discuss and approve the proposed programme, which is a result of the authority’s latest structural survey of 939 blocks out of the 1,032 blocks built more than five years ago.
The survey showed that 577 blocks have problems in the strength of the concrete, which needs repairs.
The authority has decided that 26 of them, completed between 1970 and 1974, should be demolished and redeveloped because “the strength of concrete in these blocks is so low that redevelopment will be more cost-effective than repair”.

The latest development was described as “disastrous” and “scandalous” by pressure groups on Wednesday.
Groups like the People’s Council on Public Housing Policy and the Society for Community Organisations are expected to gather residents’ opinions in the next few days and mount a campaign for improved safety on housing estates.
The 26 blocks include three blocks in Kwai Fong Estate which the authority earlier said it would redevelop following complaints of severe deterioration.
Residents of the three blocks have already started moving out.
The other blocks include another on Kwai Fong Estate, three blocks in Kwai Hing Estate, five blocks in Tsz Man, two in Kwai Shing East, two in Shek Lei, one in Tsz Oi, three in Pak Tin, one in Sau Mau Ping, three in Lam Tin, one in Wong Chuk Hang and one in Shek Pai Wan.
A government official said yesterday that although the worst affected blocks have survived several typhoons in the past years, they are below the normal standards of safety.

“They could be endangered by exceptional circumstances such as fire or freak storms,” he said.
The authority is now involved in talks with the Legal Department in cases where the structural construction of the blocks is found not to comply with original specifications.
A writ has already been filed in respect of blocks four to 11 at Kwai Fong Estate, seeking damages for breach of contract.
The estate was completed between 1971 and 1973.
The Attorney-General has instructed that no disclosure of the survey reports should be made so that the authority’s legal position is not prejudiced.
The Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC), which looked into the case earlier, found no evidence of malpractice after contacting former government officials now living overseas.

An ICAC spokesman said the investigation was completed in June.
Despite extensive inquiries both in Hong Kong and overseas, it was concluded there was insufficient evidence to substantiate a criminal prosecution, the spokesman said.
The case was subsequently presented to the Operations Review Committee whose members endorsed that all possible lines of inquiries had been exhausted and no further investigation should be undertaken by the commission.
During the investigations, ICAC officers travelled to four countries and interviewed seven former senior civil servants living there, the spokesman said.
It is believed that officers interviewed during the investigation were former senior Public Works Department officials who held supervisory positions. The government official said the latest proposal is a consequence of the Kwai Fong Estate complaints which began earlier this year.
Hundreds of families moved out earlier this year from Block 6, which is now being demolished.
More than half the 1,798 families have moved out in the past few months from the other three blocks.
The official said it has been basically agreed with the Finance Branch that the super programme should be funded as a capital expenditure item through the Development Loan Fund.
Any production arising from the programme will be in addition to the current production targets, he said.

It is planned that the production from the development will provide 11,400 flats by 1992.
However the rehousing programme, the official said, will be carried out in a long and gradual process because of the limited availability of rehousing resources.
“It is impracticable to decant the blocks with a formal notice-to-quit,” he said.
The blocks must therefore be cleared on a strict priority basis, relying on voluntary agreements between the Housing Authority and tenants to quit, which usually takes about 18 months.
The programme should also have top priority for all available rental flats, he added.
Because of the shortage of existing resources, the authority is to secure a number of surplus school sites, probably in the urban areas, for additional housing production in the coming years.
Regarding possible public reaction to the issue, the government has also considered it impracticable to immediately move tenants because although some blocks are structurally not adequate, they are not in a state of imminent failure, the official said.
“To indicate that the buildings involved are less than completely safe could be misconstrued by the public as a situation of danger and could lead to unnecessary anxiety,” he said.
A press conference is expected to be held today to explain details of the programme while discussions will also be held by district boards, district management committees, mutual aid committees and residents associations in coming weeks.
Housing Authority officials briefed Umelco members on Tuesday.
On compensation, it is proposed that domestic tenants affected by the programme should be awarded rights similar to those accorded to Mark I and II tenants in terms of removal allowances and priority for Home Ownership Scheme purchases.

It is also proposed that assessment of compensation payable to tenants of graded shops and those on fixed terms should also follow existing policies.
The details of compensation, which could cost more than $60 million, will be submitted later to the Housing Authority’s Management Committee for discussion.
The director of the People’s Council on Public Housing Policy, Mr Fung Kin-kei, described the latest development on substandard blocks as “disastrous”.
“It is probably one of the biggest scandals in recent years,” he said.
Repeating their appeal in the Kwai Fong case, Mr Fung urged the government to take action to ensure the safety of all public housing estates.
The chairman of the demolished Block 5 Mutual Aid Committee at Kwai Fong, Mr Wong Kwong-tao, said he could not believe there were so many blocks that need to be demolished.
“It’s outrageous,” he said.
